title: Today's Tech Brief: Council in UK's City of York outs hundreds of disabled residents with a single email blunder
summary: UK council's email blunder exposes disabled residents, AI's impact on serif fonts, and tech mishaps
imageAlt: Image from source article for Council in UK's City of York outs hundreds of disabled residents with a single email blunder
imageCaption: Council in UK's City of York outs hundreds of disabled residents with a single email blunder
imageSourceTitle: Council in UK's City of York outs hundreds of disabled residents with a single email blunder

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to Today's Tech Brief. We're looking at a couple of interesting stories from the world of technology. First up, a council in the UK's City of York has made a significant mistake. They accidentally exposed hundreds of disabled residents in a single email. Apparently, the council was trying to send out emails to Blue Badge holders, but they got the BCC function wrong. It's a bit like accidentally hitting 'reply all' on a sensitive email - not that I've ever done that.

The email blunder happened because the council found the BCC function too complex to use correctly. This is a classic case of a technology mishap caused by human error. The Blue Badge scheme is for people with severe mobility issues, and it's understandable that those individuals would be concerned about their information being shared. I mean, who hasn't had that moment where you meant to send a private message but ended up sharing it with the world?

In other news, it seems like AI is having an impact on the world of typography. Specifically, serif fonts are getting a bit of a makeover. According to Wired, the rise of AI is leading to a backlash against certain design choices, and serif fonts are one of the casualties. I'm not sure if I'm ready to say goodbye to my favorite fonts just yet.

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.

AI Has Come for Serif Fonts
